# Iran-Israel Conflict: Latest Developments, June 13–17, 2026

## Executive Summary

A **US-Iran framework ceasefire deal was signed on approximately June 14–15, 2026**, representing a major development in the ongoing "US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)" conflict. The deal includes a **60-day ceasefire** and commits to reopening the **Strait of Hormuz**. However, the ceasefire is **fragile and only partially holding**: Iran continues to fire drones at ships in Hormuz nightly, Israel is continuing strikes in Lebanon against Hezbollah, and Netanyahu has declared Israel will keep forces in Lebanon regardless of the deal. The deal's full text has not been publicly released, and many fundamental issues — including Iran's nuclear program — remain unresolved.

## Key Timeline of Events (June 13–17, 2026)

### June 12–13: Pre-Deal Negotiations
- **June 12**: The US declared a ceasefire deal with Iran was "very close," per NYT, with a senior administration official saying they were "not quite at the finish line yet."
- **June 13**: BBC reported Israel carrying out air strikes on Lebanon as Iran claimed a deal with the US was near.

### June 14: Deal Announced; Complications Persist
- **Trump said the US-Iran deal would be signed on Sunday (June 14)**, but Tehran cast doubt on the exact timing (BBC, June 14).
- **Israeli military struck Hezbollah targets in Beirut** on June 14, with Tehran warning this could derail the US-Iran deal (BBC).
- **Trump claimed the Strait of Hormuz would be "permanently toll-free"** under the agreement, and described Netanyahu as "a very difficult guy" (NYT, June 14).
- Israelis across the political spectrum said the agreement appeared to **leave fundamental security threats posed by Iran unaddressed** (NYT).

### June 15: Framework Deal Signed
- **US and Iran signed a framework deal** including a **60-day ceasefire** to pave the way for negotiations toward a final peace agreement and talks on Iran's nuclear program (NYT, June 15).
- **Pakistan (mediator) announced** both sides declared "the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon" (BBC, June 15).
- **Strait of Hormuz to reopen** under the deal; oil prices fell and shares jumped on the announcement (BBC).
- **Netanyahu said Israel would keep forces in Lebanon** despite the deal, declaring "The struggle has not ended" (NYT, June 15).
- **Iran claimed the deal would extend to fighting in Lebanon**, but no details were made public and Israel said its forces would remain there (NYT).
- The deal reportedly includes a **$300 billion fund for investment and reconstruction in Iran** (Times of Israel).
- **Iran said Trump had promised to suspend uranium enrichment**, but it is unclear for how long. The nuclear issue was deferred to future negotiations (NYT, BBC).
- BBC analysis: "The deal to end fighting and re-open the Strait of Hormuz leaves the sides where they were 24 hours before the war – only with thousands now dead."
- **Many Iranians expressed relief** over the agreement; opposition groups were disappointed (NYT).
- Senators were **"in the dark"** on the deal's details. Democrats demanded immediate briefing; even Republicans conceded they had no information (NYT).

### June 16: Post-Deal Challenges Emerge
- **VP Vance said Trump may release the deal text before Friday**, describing it as "about a page and a half" and "very general" (BBC, June 16).
- **Republicans again blocked a War Powers resolution** in the Senate. Four GOP senators crossed party lines, but not enough to pass the measure directing Trump to halt the war and seek congressional authorization (NYT, June 16).
- **NYT reported on a Feb. 28 US strike on an Iranian school** (Shajarah Tayyebeh elementary school in Minab) that killed at least 175 people, with months passing without answers or official acknowledgment.
- **Iran entering nuclear talks "feeling emboldened"** — despite military setbacks, Tehran is presenting a narrative of victory before negotiations (NYT).
- **Oil prices continued to fall** as traders assessed the deal, but **gas prices may remain high** due to damaged infrastructure (NYT).
- **France, Britain, and other countries announced plans to send ships** (including minesweepers) to the Strait of Hormuz to help secure it (NYT, June 16).
- **Naval mines remain a major obstacle** to reopening the strait; the US Navy deploying drone-based countermeasures to sweep the area (NYT).
- **Three reasons ships are not going through Hormuz yet**: security concerns, mines, and potential tolls (BBC, June 16).
- **Shipping companies remain cautious** about the strait (NYT).
- **Israel-Lebanon talks said to be close to yielding a lasting ceasefire deal**, though Iran threatens response if IDF keeps fighting Hezbollah (Times of Israel).
- The **Iran deal presents a "political nightmare" for Netanyahu**, trapped in a new political and security dilemma (BBC).
- **Israel kept out of loop** on US-Iran deal amid leak concerns (Times of Israel).

### June 17: Ceasefire Under Strain
- **Iran is firing multiple drones at ships in the Strait of Hormuz every night since the agreement was signed**, per NBC report cited by Times of Israel (June 17). The US is shooting down the UAVs before they threaten vessels.
- This suggests the ceasefire is **fragile and not fully holding** in practice, even if major combat operations have paused.

## Status of the Ceasefire

| Dimension | Status |
|---|---|
| **US-Iran direct military operations** | Nominally ceased under 60-day framework |
| **Strait of Hormuz** | Agreed to reopen, but not yet operational; mines, drones, security concerns persist |
| **Israel-Lebanon/Hezbollah** | Ongoing — Israel continuing strikes; separate ceasefire talks underway |
| **Iran drone activity** | Continuing nightly against ships in Hormuz |
| **Nuclear issue** | Deferred to future negotiations |
| **Deal text** | Not publicly released as of June 17 |

## Context: The Broader Conflict

- The NYT categorizes this as the **"US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)"** and the **"Iran-Israel War (2025–)"**, indicating the conflict began in 2025.
- The war involved **US military strikes on Iranian territory**, including the devastating Feb. 28 strike on a school.
- Iran **blockaded the Strait of Hormuz** during the conflict, trapping ~20,000 seafarers and causing a global energy crisis.
- **Gulf countries** (Bahrain, Qatar, UAE) were targeted by Iran during the war, damaging their economies and raising questions about reliance on US protection.
- The **2026 FIFA World Cup** is concurrent, with Iran's team playing in the US under extraordinarily tense circumstances.

## Assessment of Ceasefire Durability

The ceasefire is **formally in place but practically strained**:
- **Positive signals**: Major combat operations between US and Iran appear to have paused; oil markets reacting favorably; diplomatic channels open; G7 allies supporting the deal.
- **Negative signals**: Iran firing drones nightly at Hormuz shipping; Israel continuing operations in Lebanon; deal text not released; nuclear issue unresolved; Congress not consulted; Netanyahu publicly defiant; mines and security obstacles preventing Hormuz from actually reopening.
- The deal is described as **"very general"** (per VP Vance), approximately "a page and a half," with most contentious issues deferred to future negotiations.

key_facts: A US-Iran framework ceasefire deal was signed around June 14-15, 2026, including a 60-day ceasefire and commitment to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
Pakistan mediated the deal; both sides declared 'the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon.'
The deal text has not been publicly released as of June 17, 2026; VP Vance described it as 'about a page and a half' and 'very general.'
Iran is firing multiple drones at ships in the Strait of Hormuz every night since the agreement was signed, with US forces shooting them down (Times of Israel/NBC, June 17).
Netanyahu declared Israel will keep forces in Lebanon despite the deal, saying 'The struggle has not ended' (NYT, June 15).
Israel continued striking Hezbollah targets in Beirut on June 14, with Tehran warning this could derail the deal.
The deal reportedly includes a $300 billion fund for investment and reconstruction in Iran (Times of Israel).
Iran's nuclear program was deferred to future negotiations; Trump said Iran promised to suspend uranium enrichment but duration is unclear.
Naval mines, security concerns, and potential tolls are preventing ships from transiting the Strait of Hormuz despite the agreement (BBC, NYT).
France, Britain, and other countries announced plans to send minesweepers and ships to help secure the Strait of Hormuz (NYT, June 16).
US Republicans blocked a War Powers resolution in the Senate on June 16; four GOP senators crossed party lines but it was insufficient.
A Feb. 28, 2026 US strike on an Iranian elementary school in Minab killed at least 175 people; no official US acknowledgment months later (NYT).
Israel-Lebanon ceasefire talks are reportedly close to yielding a lasting deal, separate from the US-Iran framework (Times of Israel, June 16).
Oil prices fell and stock markets rallied following the deal announcement (BBC, NYT).
The conflict is categorized by NYT as the 'US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)' and 'Iran-Israel War (2025-)'.
Israel was kept out of the loop on the US-Iran deal amid leak concerns (Times of Israel).
~20,000 seafarers were trapped by the Strait of Hormuz blockade during the conflict (BBC).
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is concurrent, with Iran's team playing in the US under tense circumstances.
